title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Note



This preregistration has not yet been registered on the OSF and may change within the next days.

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Hypotheses
H1 — Association between alienation and trust: Perceptions of alienation are negatively associated with trust in climate scientists.
H2 — Epistemic autonomy as moderator of the alienation-trust relationship: Climate scientists claim epistemic authority over matters of public concern. People with a high need for epistemic autonomy, who are motivated to form their own judgments rather than defer to authorities, should be particularly distrusting of climate scientists when they experience alienation. We therefore predict that need for epistemic autonomy moderates the relationship between alienation and distrust, such that the alienation–distrust association is stronger among high-autonomy individuals.
For both hypotheses, we test the four dimensions of alienation separately.

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Research Questions
RQ1 — Structural predictors of institutional and social alienation: Do people who are demographically underrepresented among climate scientists—by race, education, gender, or social class—report higher institutional and social alienation? This tests whether self-reported perceptions of alienation map onto objective indicators of structural underrepresentation.
RQ2 — Structural predictors of spatial alienation: Do people who live further from scientific institutions—operationalized as geographic distance from the nearest research university—report higher spatial alienation? This tests whether geographic distance predicts perceived distance.
RQ3 — Alienation and dimensions of trust: Alienated people may still recognize climate scientists’ technical competence, but have more reason to doubt their benevolence, integrity, and openness—dimensions of trust that reflect whether an institution acts in one’s interest rather than merely whether it is capable. We therefore ask whether alienation is more strongly associated with distrust along the dimensions of benevolence, integrity, and openness than along the dimension of competence.
RQ4 — Alienation and policy role: People who are alienated from climate scientists may be more resistant to granting scientists a role in shaping public policy. We ask whether alienation predicts opposition to scientists having influence over climate policy.

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Source
We use pre-treatment data from the Strengthening trust in climate scientists megastudy (N ≈ 22,000), a large collaborative experiment testing 20 interventions to strengthen trust in climate scientists in the United States. Participants are recruited from a national, non-probability opt-in panel (CloudResearch) with cross-quotas on gender × age and gender × race based on 2024 US Census Bureau population estimates.
This project presents correlational analyses of the megastudy pre-treatment measures. No experimental comparisons are made. No data has been collected yet.

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Ethics
The megastudy has received ethics approval from Stanford University (IRB-85756) and ETH Zurich (26 ETHICS-093).

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Alienation from climate science
Except for informational alienation, all items use a 7-point agree/disagree response scale (1 = strongly disagree, 7 = strongly agree). Higher scores indicate greater alienation. We will use composite scores (the mean) of all items present in one dimension.

Institutional alienation (2 items)

The prospect of working as a climate scientist has always seemed beyond my reach.
Careers in climate research are accessible only to a privileged few.



Social alienation (2 items)

Climate scientists have a different social background than me.
Climate scientists move in different social circles than me.



Spatial alienation (2 items)

Climate science has no positive impact on my local area.
Very few climate scientists live or work in my local area.



Informational alienation (6 items)
“How often do you see or hear information about climate change in the following places?”

Traditional media (e.g., newspapers, TV, radio)
Online news (e.g., news websites, podcasts, YouTube)
Social media (e.g., Facebook, TikTok, Instagram)
Fiction (e.g., films, series, books, comics)
Personal conversations (e.g., talking with friends or family, text messages, messaging apps)
In-person events (e.g., museums, public talks)

Response options: 1 = Never, 2 = Rarely, 3 = Occasionally, 4 = Frequently, 5 = Very frequently. Items are reverse-coded before computing the composite, so that higher scores indicate less exposure (more informational alienation).

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Trust in climate scientists

Single-item trust
The primary outcome is a single outcome trust measure, assessed pre-treatment in the megastudy.
“How much do you trust climate scientists?” (slider, 0–100).

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Multidimensional trust (secondary outcome, 12 items)
For a robustness check, we use a more detailed 12-item trust scale covering four subdimensions (3 items each), assessed on 0–100 sliders (0 = most negative, 100 = most positive); see Table 1. Subdimension composites are computed as the mean of the three items within each subdimension. The overall composite (trust_multidimensional) is the mean of all 12 items.
The reason this scale is used only for a robustness check is that, in the megastudy, it is asked “post-treatment”. We can therefore only use answers from the control group for unconfounded results.




Table 1: Multidimensional trust scale: subdimensions and bipolar item anchors. Each item is rated on a 0–100 slider.





    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                Subdimension
                Items (bipolar anchors)
              
        
        
        
                
                  Competence
                  incompetent–competent; unintelligent–intelligent; unqualified–qualified
                
                
                  Integrity
                  dishonest–honest; unethical–ethical; insincere–sincere
                
                
                  Benevolence
                  unconcerned–concerned; uneager–eager to improve lives; inconsiderate–considerate
                
                
                  Openness
                  not open–open to feedback; unwilling–willing to be transparent; little–great attention to others' views

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Need for epistemic autonomy (6 items)
[Beebe et al. (2019); adapted]
“Please indicate how much you agree or disagree with the following statements” (1 = strongly disagree, 7 = strongly agree):

I like to think things through for myself.
I like to figure things out for myself.
I like to make up my own mind about things.
I only believe something if I can see for myself that it is true.
I don’t go along with the opinions of others without thinking things through for myself.
I have never really questioned the things I have been taught to believe. [reverse-scored]

We will use a composite measure (mean) of all items items, reverse-coding item 6.

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Structural indicators (for RQ1 and RQ2)

Race/ethnicity: White, Black, Hispanic, Asian, Other (categorical)
Education: 6-level ordinal (less than high school → doctorate)
Gender: Male, Female, Other
Income: 5-level ordinal (<$30k to ≥$168k)
Geographic distance (dist_univ_km): distance (km) from the participant’s ZIP code to the nearest R1 research university, computed using ZIP-code centroid coordinates

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Policy role of scientists (for RQ4)
Four items assessing support for scientists having a role in climate policy (e.g., collaborating with policymakers, advocating for policies, communicating findings; 0–100 sliders). We will use a composite score (mean) of all items.

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Overview
All models are estimated using OLS regression with heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors (HC2, via the sandwich package). All continuous predictors and outcomes are standardized (z-scored) before model fitting, so reported coefficients are standardized betas and directly comparable across dimensions. Within each hypothesis and research question, multiple comparisons are corrected using the Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) false discovery rate procedure (Benjamini and Hochberg 1995).
Code chunks below demonstrate each analysis on simulated data. Function definitions are printed before their first use so the exact model specification is part of the preregistration record.

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
section: H1: Association between alienation and trust
text:
H1: Association between alienation and trust
For each of the four alienation dimensions, we run separate regressions. We regress the single-item pre-treatment trust measure (trust_pre) on the respective alienation composite. We do not include covariates. BH correction is applied across the four tests (one per dimension).
run_ols_model <- function(data,

alienation_dims <- c(
  "alien_inst_mean", "alien_social_mean",
  "alien_spatial_mean", "alien_info_mean"
)

h1_results <- map_dfr(alienation_dims, \(pred)
  run_ols_model(data, outcome = "trust_pre", predictor = pred)
) |>
  mutate(p_adjusted = p.adjust(p.value, method = "BH"))

h1_results |>
  select(predictor, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value, p_adjusted, n) |>
  mutate(predictor = str_remove(predictor, "_mean") |>
           str_remove("alien_") |> str_to_title()) |>
  rounded_numbers() |>
  rename(
    Dimension = predictor, Beta = estimate, SE = std.error,
    t = statistic, p = p.value, `p (BH-adj)` = p_adjusted, N = n
  ) |>
  tt(caption = "H1: Effect of alienation dimensions on single-item trust (standardized beta, HC2 SEs)")



    

    

    
    
    
      
        H1: Effect of alienation dimensions on single-item trust (standardized beta, HC2 SEs)
        
              
                Dimension
                Beta
                SE
                t
                p
                p (BH-adj)
                N
              
        
        
        
                
                  Inst
                  -0.053
                  0.043
                  -1.227
                  0.220
                  0.881
                  500
                
                
                  Social
                  0.002
                  0.044
                  0.046
                  0.963
                  0.963
                  500
                
                
                  Spatial
                  -0.030
                  0.045
                  -0.677
                  0.499
                  0.963
                  500
                
                
                  Info
                  -0.007
                  0.043
                  -0.151
                  0.880

                  500
                
        
      
    








H1: Standardized associations between alienation dimensions and single-item trust (trust_pre). Colored points: BH-adjusted p < .05.

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
H2: Epistemic autonomy as moderator
For each alienation dimension, we estimate an interaction model:
\[
\text{trust\_pre} \sim \text{alienation}_d \times \text{epist\_auton\_mean} + \text{age} + \text{gender} + \text{race}
\]
We report the interaction coefficient (alienation × epistemic autonomy) for each dimension, with BH correction across the four interaction tests.
run_interaction_model <- function(data,

h2_results <- map_dfr(alienation_dims, \(pred)
  run_interaction_model(
    data,
    outcome    = "trust_pre",
    predictor  = pred,
    moderator  = "epist_auton_mean",
    covariates = c("age", "gender", "race")
  )
) |>
  filter(str_detect(term, ":")) |>
  mutate(p_adjusted = p.adjust(p.value, method = "BH"))

h2_results |>
  select(predictor, moderator, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value, p_adjusted, n) |>
  mutate(predictor = str_remove(predictor, "_mean") |>
           str_remove("alien_") |> str_to_title()) |>
  rounded_numbers() |>
  rename(
    `Alienation dim.` = predictor, Moderator = moderator,
    Beta = estimate, SE = std.error, t = statistic,
    p = p.value, `p (BH-adj)` = p_adjusted, N = n
  ) |>
  tt(caption = "H2: Interaction of each alienation dimension with epistemic autonomy predicting trust_pre")



    

    

    
    
    
      
        H2: Interaction of each alienation dimension with epistemic autonomy predicting trust_pre
        
              
                Alienation dim.
                Moderator
                Beta
                SE
                t
                p
                p (BH-adj)
                N
              
        
        
        
                
                  Inst
                  epist_auton_mean
                  0.039
                  0.048
                  0.824
                  0.410
                  0.974
                  500
                
                
                  Social
                  epist_auton_mean
                  0.026
                  0.050
                  0.526
                  0.599
                  0.974
                  500
                
                
                  Spatial
                  epist_auton_mean
                  -0.002
                  0.047
                  -0.032
                  0.974

                  500
                
                
                  Info
                  epist_auton_mean
                  0.003
                  0.047

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
section: RQ1: Structural predictors of institutional and social alienation
text:
RQ1: Structural predictors of institutional and social alienation
We regress each alienation outcome (alien_inst_mean, alien_social_mean) separately on each structural predictor: race (White as reference), education, gender (Male as reference), and social class (Middle class as reference). Each predictor is entered in a separate model that adjusts for age and the remaining demographic covariates. BH correction is applied within each alienation outcome across all structural predictors.

structural_preds <- c("race", "education", "gender", "social_class")
rq1_outcomes     <- c("alien_inst_mean", "alien_social_mean")

rq1_results <- map_dfr(rq1_outcomes, \(out)
  map_dfr(structural_preds, \(pred)
    run_ols_model(
      data,
      outcome    = out,
      predictor  = pred,
      covariates = c("age", setdiff(structural_preds, pred))
    )
  ) |>
    mutate(p_adjusted = p.adjust(p.value, method = "BH"),
           outcome    = out)
)

rq1_results |>
  select(outcome, predictor, term, estimate, std.error, p.value, p_adjusted) |>
  mutate(
    outcome   = str_remove(outcome, "_mean") |> str_remove("alien_") |> str_to_title(),
    predictor = str_replace_all(predictor, "_", " ") |> str_to_title(),
    term      = str_replace_all(term, "_", " ")
  ) |>
  rounded_numbers() |>
  rename(
    Outcome = outcome, Variable = predictor, Level = term,
    Beta = estimate, SE = std.error, p = p.value, `p (BH-adj)` = p_adjusted
  ) |>
  tt(caption = "RQ1: Structural predictors of institutional and social alienation")

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
section: RQ2: Structural predictor of spatial alienation
text:
RQ2: Structural predictor of spatial alienation
We regress spatial alienation on log-transformed geographic distance to the nearest research university, adjusting for age, gender, race, and education. This is a single test; no BH correction needed.

rq2_result <- run_ols_model(
  data |> mutate(log_dist_univ_km = log(dist_univ_km)),
  outcome    = "alien_spatial_mean",
  predictor  = "log_dist_univ_km",
  covariates = c("age", "gender", "race", "education")
)

rq2_result |>
  select(predictor, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value, n) |>
  mutate(predictor = "log(distance to university, km)") |>
  rounded_numbers() |>
  rename(Predictor = predictor, Beta = estimate, SE = std.error,
         t = statistic, p = p.value, N = n) |>
  tt(caption = "RQ2: Geographic distance predicting spatial alienation")



    

    

    
    
    
      
        RQ2: Geographic distance predicting spatial alienation
        
              
                Predictor
                Beta
                SE
                t
                p
                N
              
        
        
        
                
                  log(distance to university, km)
                  0.083
                  0.044
                  1.914
                  0.056
                  500

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
section: RQ3: Alienation and dimensions of trust
text:
RQ3: Alienation and dimensions of trust
The multidimensional trust scale is measured post-treatment in the megastudy. To obtain unconfounded estimates, we restrict this analysis to control group participants only. For each of the four alienation dimensions, we regress each trust subdimension separately and apply BH correction across the four subdimension tests within each alienation dimension.

trust_dims  <- c("trust_competence", "trust_integrity",
                 "trust_benevolence", "trust_openness")
data_control <- data |> filter(condition == "control")

rq3_results <- map_dfr(alienation_dims, \(pred)
  map_dfr(trust_dims, \(out)
    run_ols_model(data_control, outcome = out, predictor = pred)
  ) |>
    mutate(p_adjusted = p.adjust(p.value, method = "BH"))
)

rq3_results |>
  filter(predictor == "alien_inst_mean") |>    # example: institutional alienation
  select(outcome, estimate, std.error, p.value, p_adjusted) |>
  mutate(outcome = str_remove(outcome, "trust_") |> str_to_title()) |>
  rounded_numbers() |>
  rename(Dimension = outcome, Beta = estimate, SE = std.error,
         p = p.value, `p (BH-adj)` = p_adjusted) |>
  tt(caption = "RQ3 (example: institutional alienation): Effect on each trust subdimension (control group only)")

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
RQ4: Alienation and policy role
We regress policy role support (policy_role_mean) on each alienation dimension separately, without covariates (mirroring H1). BH correction across four tests.

rq4_results <- map_dfr(alienation_dims, \(pred)
  run_ols_model(data, outcome = "policy_role_mean", predictor = pred)
) |>
  mutate(p_adjusted = p.adjust(p.value, method = "BH"))

rq4_results |>
  select(predictor, estimate, std.error, p.value, p_adjusted, n) |>
  mutate(predictor = str_remove(predictor, "_mean") |>
           str_remove("alien_") |> str_to_title()) |>
  rounded_numbers() |>
  rename(
    Dimension = predictor, Beta = estimate, SE = std.error,
    p = p.value, `p (BH-adj)` = p_adjusted, N = n
  ) |>
  tt(caption = "RQ4: Alienation dimensions predicting policy role support")

title: Preregistration: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists in the US
text:
Scale Reliability

get_alpha <- function(df, cols) {
  psych::alpha(df[, cols], check.keys = FALSE)$total$raw_alpha
}

tibble(
  Scale = c(
    "Institutional alienation",
    "Social alienation",
    "Spatial alienation",
    "Informational alienation",
    "Trust: Competence",
    "Trust: Integrity",
    "Trust: Benevolence",
    "Trust: Openness",
    "Trust: Multidimensional (all 12 items)",
    "Epistemic autonomy"
  ),
  Items = c(2, 2, 2, 6, 3, 3, 3, 3, 12, 6),
  Alpha = c(
    get_alpha(data, c("alien_inst_1", "alien_inst_2")),
    get_alpha(data, c("alien_social_1", "alien_social_2")),
    get_alpha(data, c("alien_spatial_1", "alien_spatial_2")),
    get_alpha(data, paste0("alien_info_", 1:6, "_r")),
    get_alpha(data, paste0("trust_competence_", 1:3)),
    get_alpha(data, paste0("trust_integrity_", 1:3)),
    get_alpha(data, paste0("trust_benevolence_", 1:3)),
    get_alpha(data, paste0("trust_openness_", 1:3)),
    get_alpha(data, c(paste0("trust_competence_", 1:3),
                      paste0("trust_integrity_", 1:3),
                      paste0("trust_benevolence_", 1:3),
                      paste0("trust_openness_", 1:3))),
    get_alpha(data, c("epist_auton_1", "epist_auton_2", "epist_auton_3",
                      "epist_auton_4", "epist_auton_5", "epist_auton_6r"))
  )
) |>
  mutate(Alpha = round(Alpha, 2)) |>
  tt(caption = "Internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha) of all multi-item scales (simulated data)")

Some items ( alien_info_5_r ) were negatively correlated with the first principal component and 
probably should be reversed.  
To do this, run the function again with the 'check.keys=TRUE' option

title: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists
text:
This project provides the first direct, individual-level test of the alienation model of science distrust, applied to trust in climate scientists.
Prominent psychological accounts attribute distrust in scientists to cognitive deficiencies — lack of knowledge, motivated reasoning, or conspiracist thinking. We test a structural alternative: the alienation model (Gauchat 2012), which holds that distrust stems from perceived exclusion from scientific institutions rather than individual irrationality. People who feel structurally excluded — institutionally, socially, geographically, or informationally — have less influence over scientific institutions, which may rationally translate into greater wariness.
We derive and test specific individual-level predictions of the alienation model using data from the Trust-In Megastudy (N ≈ 22,000, US adults). All analyses are pre-registered correlational tests conducted on survey data collected as part of the megastudy.

title: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists
text:
This project provides the first direct, individual-level test of the alienation model of science distrust, applied to trust in climate scientists.
Prominent psychological accounts attribute distrust in scientists to cognitive deficiencies — lack of knowledge, motivated reasoning, or conspiracist thinking. We test a structural alternative: the alienation model (Gauchat 2012), which holds that distrust stems from perceived exclusion from scientific institutions rather than individual irrationality. People who feel structurally excluded — institutionally, socially, geographically, or informationally — have less influence over scientific institutions, which may rationally translate into greater wariness.
We derive and test specific individual-level predictions of the alienation model using data from the Trust-In Megastudy (N ≈ 22,000, US adults). All analyses are pre-registered correlational tests conducted on survey data collected as part of the megastudy.

title: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists
text:
Key questions

Do people who feel more alienated from climate science distrust climate scientists more? (H1)
Does this association strengthen among those with a high need for epistemic autonomy? (H2)
Do structurally underrepresented groups — by race, education, gender, income — report higher institutional and social alienation? (RQ1)
Do people living farther from scientific institutions report higher spatial alienation? (RQ2)
Is alienation more strongly linked to distrust along the dimensions of benevolence, integrity, and openness than competence? (RQ3)
Do alienated people more strongly oppose scientists having a role in shaping public policy? (RQ4)

title: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists
text:
Data
Data come from the Trust-In Megastudy, a large collaborative experiment testing 20 interventions to strengthen trust in climate scientists. The megastudy was conducted on a national US sample via CloudResearch (N ≈ 22,000). This project uses only the pre-treatment measures and correlates them; no experimental comparisons are made.

title: Alienation and Trust in Climate Scientists
text:
Pre-registration
This project is pre-registered. See the preregistration document for full hypotheses, measures, and analysis plan.

title: Scientific Institutions: Location Data & ZIP-Code Distance Pipeline
text:
This document prepares the dist_inst_km variable used in the alienation–trust preregistration: the geodesic distance (km) from each participant’s ZIP code to the nearest scientific institution.
What counts as a scientific institution?
We include two categories:

Research universities — Carnegie R1 (Doctoral: Very High Research Activity) and R2 (Doctoral: High Research Activity) institutions, sourced from the Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System (IPEDS) via the Urban Institute’s educationdata package.
Major federal research labs — approximately 50 facilities operated by DOE, NIH, NIST, NOAA, NASA, and USGS, with coordinates sourced from each agency’s website and verified against OpenStreetMap.

Why log-transform? Geographic distance is right-skewed (a few very remote participants drive the distribution). All analyses use log(dist_inst_km).
Outputs (saved to data/institutions/, gitignored):

institutions.rds — clean institution dataset with coordinates
zipcode_distances.rds — ZIP-code lookup table: one row per ZIP, with nearest institution and distance in km

title: Scientific Institutions: Location Data & ZIP-Code Distance Pipeline
text:
This document prepares the dist_inst_km variable used in the alienation–trust preregistration: the geodesic distance (km) from each participant’s ZIP code to the nearest scientific institution.
What counts as a scientific institution?
We include two categories:

Research universities — Carnegie R1 (Doctoral: Very High Research Activity) and R2 (Doctoral: High Research Activity) institutions, sourced from the Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System (IPEDS) via the Urban Institute’s educationdata package.
Major federal research labs — approximately 50 facilities operated by DOE, NIH, NIST, NOAA, NASA, and USGS, with coordinates sourced from each agency’s website and verified against OpenStreetMap.

Why log-transform? Geographic distance is right-skewed (a few very remote participants drive the distribution). All analyses use log(dist_inst_km).
Outputs (saved to data/institutions/, gitignored):

institutions.rds — clean institution dataset with coordinates
zipcode_distances.rds — ZIP-code lookup table: one row per ZIP, with nearest institution and distance in km

title: Scientific Institutions: Location Data & ZIP-Code Distance Pipeline
text:
Setup


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(here)
library(sf)
library(geosphere)
library(zipcodeR)




Code
dir.create(here("data", "institutions"), recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

title: Scientific Institutions: Location Data & ZIP-Code Distance Pipeline
text:
Part 1: Research Universities (IPEDS)
We download the IPEDS Header/Directory file (HD2022) directly from NCES. This file is the authoritative source for institution characteristics including coordinates and Carnegie classification codes. Downloading directly avoids any API dependency.
Carnegie classification codes of interest (variable C18BASIC in IPEDS):



Code
Label




15
Doctoral Universities: Very High Research Activity (R1)


16
Doctoral Universities: High Research Activity (R2)





Code
# Download IPEDS HD2022 (Header/Directory) — ~3 MB zip, ~6,000 institutions.
# Cached by knitr after first run.
ipeds_zip  <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
ipeds_dir  <- tempdir()

download.file(
  url      = "https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/datacenter/data/HD2022.zip",
  destfile = ipeds_zip,
  quiet    = TRUE,
  mode     = "wb"
)

unzip(ipeds_zip, exdir = ipeds_dir)

# The main CSV is lowercase in the zip; find it regardless of case
csv_file   <- list.files(ipeds_dir, pattern = "(?i)^hd2022\\.csv$",
                         full.names = TRUE)[1]
ipeds_raw  <- read_csv(csv_file, show_col_types = FALSE)

# Key columns: INSTNM, CITY, STABBR, LATITUDE, LONGITUD, C18BASIC
glimpse(ipeds_raw |> select(INSTNM, CITY, STABBR, LATITUDE, LONGITUD, C18BASIC))


Rows: 6,256
Columns: 6
$ INSTNM   <chr> "Alabama A & M University", "University of Alabama at Birming…
$ CITY     <chr> "Normal", "Birmingham", "Montgomery", "Huntsville", "Montgome…
$ STABBR   <chr> "AL", "AL", "AL", "AL", "AL", "AL", "AL", "AL", "AL", "AL", "…
$ LATITUDE <dbl> 34.78337, 33.50570, 32.36261, 34.72456, 32.36432, 33.20701, 3…
$ LONGITUD <dbl> -86.56850, -86.79935, -86.17401, -86.64045, -86.29568, -87.52…
$ C18BASIC <dbl> 18, 15, 20, 16, 19, -2, 15, 2, 22, 18, 15, 21, 1, 20, 5, 5, 1…




Code
universities <- ipeds_raw |>
  filter(
    C18BASIC %in% c(15, 16),        # R1 and R2 only
    !is.na(LATITUDE),
    !is.na(LONGITUD),
    # Drop non-contiguous US (Alaska, Hawaii, territories)
    LATITUDE  > 24 & LATITUDE  < 50,
    LONGITUD  > -125 & LONGITUD < -66
  ) |>
  transmute(
    name             = INSTNM,
    city             = CITY,
    state_abbr       = STABBR,
    lat              = LATITUDE,
    lon              = LONGITUD,
    carnegie_class   = if_else(C18BASIC == 15, "R1", "R2"),
    institution_type = "university"
  )

cat("R1 universities:", sum(universities$carnegie_class == "R1"), "\n")


R1 universities: 131 


Code
cat("R2 universities:", sum(universities$carnegie_class == "R2"), "\n")


R2 universities: 131 


Code
cat("Total:          ", nrow(universities), "\n")


Total:           262 




Code
universities_sf <- st_as_sf(universities, coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = universities_sf,
          aes(color = carnegie_class), size = 1.2, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(R1 = "#2c7bb6", R2 = "#abd9e9"),
                     name = "Carnegie") +
  labs(title = "Research universities included (R1 + R2)",
       caption = "Source: IPEDS 2022 via Urban Institute educationdata package") +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Scientific Institutions: Location Data & ZIP-Code Distance Pipeline
text:
Part 2: Federal Research Labs
Manually curated list of ~50 major federal research facilities. Coordinates verified against agency websites and OpenStreetMap. This list intentionally covers the largest, most prominent labs — it is not exhaustive of every federal facility, but captures the institutions most relevant to structural proximity to organized science.


Code
federal_labs <- tribble(
  ~name,                                          ~agency,  ~lat,     ~lon,
  # ── Department of Energy national labs ─────────────────────────────────
  "Argonne National Laboratory",                  "DOE",   41.7059, -87.9837,
  "Brookhaven National Laboratory",               "DOE",   40.8674, -72.8776,
  "Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory",        "DOE",   41.8319, -88.2617,
  "Idaho National Laboratory",                    "DOE",   43.5246,-112.9541,
  "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory",        "DOE",   37.8755,-122.2477,
  "Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory",       "DOE",   37.6868,-121.7051,
  "Los Alamos National Laboratory",               "DOE",   35.8800,-106.2951,
  "National Renewable Energy Laboratory",         "DOE",   39.7405,-105.1686,
  "Oak Ridge National Laboratory",                "DOE",   35.9310, -84.3100,
  "Pacific Northwest National Laboratory",        "DOE",   46.3440,-119.2785,
  "Sandia National Laboratories (NM)",            "DOE",   34.9653,-106.3969,
  "Sandia National Laboratories (CA)",            "DOE",   37.9717,-121.7046,
  "SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory",         "DOE",   37.4160,-122.2034,
  "Ames National Laboratory",                     "DOE",   42.0267, -93.7317,
  "Jefferson Lab",                                "DOE",   37.0977, -76.4791,
  "Savannah River National Laboratory",           "DOE",   33.3487, -81.7229,
  "National Energy Technology Laboratory",        "DOE",   39.9279, -79.9701,
  # ── National Institutes of Health ──────────────────────────────────────
  "NIH Main Campus",                              "NIH",   39.0007, -77.1055,
  "NIH Rocky Mountain Labs",                      "NIH",   46.8574,-113.9867,
  "NIH Research Triangle Park",                   "NIH",   35.8973, -78.8622,
  # ── National Institute of Standards and Technology ──────────────────────
  "NIST Gaithersburg",                            "NIST",  39.1330, -77.2177,
  "NIST Boulder",                                 "NIST",  40.0050,-105.2625,
  # ── NOAA research labs ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
  "NOAA Earth System Research Lab (Boulder)",     "NOAA",  39.9950,-105.2639,
  "NOAA Geophysical Fluid Dynamics Lab",          "NOAA",  40.3502, -74.6573,
  "NOAA Atlantic Oceanographic Lab (Miami)",      "NOAA",  25.7306, -80.1625,
  "NOAA Pacific Marine Environmental Lab",        "NOAA",  47.6456,-122.3130,
  "NOAA National Severe Storms Lab",              "NOAA",  35.1817, -97.4432,
  "NOAA Great Lakes Environmental Research Lab",  "NOAA",  42.2986, -83.7082,
  # ── NASA field centers ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
  "NASA Ames Research Center",                    "NASA",  37.4094,-122.0640,
  "NASA Armstrong Flight Research Center",        "NASA",  34.9594,-117.8822,
  "NASA Glenn Research Center",                   "NASA",  41.4273, -81.8633,
  "NASA Goddard Space Flight Center",             "NASA",  38.9915, -76.8404,
  "NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory",               "NASA",  34.2014,-118.1720,
  "NASA Johnson Space Center",                    "NASA",  29.5593, -95.0938,
  "NASA Kennedy Space Center",                    "NASA",  28.5729, -80.6490,
  "NASA Langley Research Center",                 "NASA",  37.0837, -76.3694,
  "NASA Marshall Space Flight Center",            "NASA",  34.7293, -86.6650,
  "NASA Stennis Space Center",                    "NASA",  30.3629, -89.6003,
  # ── USGS science centers ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
  "USGS National Center (Reston)",                "USGS",  38.9476, -77.3673,
  "USGS Western Region (Menlo Park)",             "USGS",  37.4574,-122.1730,
  "USGS Central Region (Denver)",                 "USGS",  39.7543,-104.8797,
  "USGS Woods Hole Coastal Science Center",       "USGS",  41.5250, -70.6710,
  "USGS Columbia Environmental Research Center",  "USGS",  38.9387, -92.3341,
  "USGS Patuxent Wildlife Research Center",       "USGS",  38.9980, -76.8155,
  # ── CDC / EPA ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  "CDC / ATSDR (Atlanta)",                        "CDC",   33.7993, -84.3277,
  "EPA Office of Research & Development (RTP)",   "EPA",   35.8905, -78.8751,
  "EPA National Risk Management Lab (Cincinnati)","EPA",   39.1290, -84.5122
) |>
  mutate(institution_type = "federal_lab",
         carnegie_class   = NA_character_,
         city             = NA_character_,
         state_abbr       = NA_character_)

cat("Federal labs:", nrow(federal_labs), "\n")


Federal labs: 47 




Code
labs_sf <- st_as_sf(federal_labs, coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = labs_sf, aes(color = agency), size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Federal research labs included",
       color = "Agency",
       caption = "Coordinates verified against agency websites and OpenStreetMap") +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Scientific Institutions: Location Data & ZIP-Code Distance Pipeline
text:
Part 3: Combined Institution Dataset


Code
institutions <- bind_rows(
  universities |> select(name, city, state_abbr, lat, lon,
                          carnegie_class, institution_type),
  federal_labs |> select(name, city, state_abbr, lat, lon,
                          carnegie_class, institution_type)
)

institutions |>
  count(institution_type, carnegie_class) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Institution counts by type")



Institution counts by type


institution_type
carnegie_class
n




federal_lab
NA
47


university
R1
131


university
R2
131







Code
inst_sf <- st_as_sf(institutions, coords = c("lon", "lat"), crs = 4326)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = inst_sf,
          aes(color = institution_type, size = institution_type),
          alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(university = "#2c7bb6", federal_lab = "#d7191c"),
                     name = NULL) +
  scale_size_manual(values  = c(university = 0.8, federal_lab = 2),
                    guide   = "none") +
  labs(title    = "All scientific institutions",
       subtitle = paste0(sum(institutions$institution_type == "university"),
                         " R1/R2 universities + ",
                         sum(institutions$institution_type == "federal_lab"),
                         " federal labs")) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")











Code
saveRDS(institutions, here("data", "institutions", "institutions.rds"))
cat("Saved institutions.rds:", nrow(institutions), "rows\n")


Saved institutions.rds: 309 rows

title: Scientific Institutions: Location Data & ZIP-Code Distance Pipeline
text:
Part 4: ZIP Code Distance Pipeline
For each US ZIP code, we compute the geodesic distance (km) to the nearest institution using the Haversine formula. ZIP-code centroids come from zipcodeR::zip_code_db, which covers all ~40,000 US ZIP codes with no API calls required.
We restrict to the continental US (latitude 24°–50°, longitude −125° to −66°) to match the participant sample and the institution coverage above.


Code
zips <- zip_code_db |>
  filter(
    !is.na(lat), !is.na(lng),
    lat  > 24 & lat  < 50,
    lng > -125 & lng < -66
  ) |>
  select(zip_code = zipcode, zip_lat = lat, zip_lon = lng)

cat("ZIP codes in continental US:", nrow(zips), "\n")


ZIP codes in continental US: 32657 




Code
# Convert to sf for fast nearest-neighbor search
zips_sf  <- st_as_sf(zips, coords = c("zip_lon", "zip_lat"), crs = 4326)
inst_sf2 <- st_as_sf(institutions, coords = c("lon", "lat"),  crs = 4326)

# Find index of nearest institution for each ZIP code
nearest_idx <- st_nearest_feature(zips_sf, inst_sf2)

# Retrieve institution metadata for each matched row
nearest_inst <- institutions[nearest_idx, ] |>
  select(nearest_institution = name, institution_type)

# Compute Haversine distance (km) between each ZIP centroid and its nearest institution
inst_coords <- cbind(institutions$lon[nearest_idx],
                     institutions$lat[nearest_idx])
zip_coords  <- cbind(zips$zip_lon, zips$zip_lat)

dist_km <- distHaversine(zip_coords, inst_coords) / 1000

zipcode_distances <- bind_cols(zips, nearest_inst) |>
  mutate(dist_inst_km = dist_km)

head(zipcode_distances)


  zip_code zip_lat zip_lon                 nearest_institution institution_type
1    35004   33.62  -86.50 University of Alabama at Birmingham       university
2    35005   33.59  -86.99 University of Alabama at Birmingham       university
3    35006   33.40  -87.20           The University of Alabama       university
4    35007   33.22  -86.79 University of Alabama at Birmingham       university
5    35010   32.90  -85.90                   Auburn University       university
6    35013   33.90  -86.40 University of Alabama at Birmingham       university
  dist_inst_km
1     30.54386
2     20.02362
3     38.40107
4     31.81551
5     51.04835
6     57.39679



Validation


Code
summary(zipcode_distances$dist_inst_km)


    Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
  0.0291  28.7875  62.2344  77.5828 103.8492 581.0799 




Code
# NYC (10001) → should be near 0; rural Montana (59931) → should be large
zipcode_distances |>
  filter(zip_code %in% c("10001", "59931", "90210", "60601", "73301")) |>
  select(zip_code, nearest_institution, institution_type, dist_inst_km) |>
  mutate(dist_inst_km = round(dist_inst_km, 1)) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Spot checks: selected ZIP codes")

  geom_histogram(bins = 80, fill = "#2c7bb6", color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::comma) +

    x        = "Distance to nearest institution (km, log scale)",
    y        = "Number of ZIP codes",

    subtitle = "Right-skewed → log-transform used in analysis"
